title: WBS applicability across all project types
overview: Explains why the WBS is relevant to every project type.
detailed_summary: The common thread across all projects is that every project produces deliverables. Therefore, the WBS is applicable to and required for every project because all projects implement changes to provide new or different outcomes for customers, whether internal or external. These outcomes may be delivered as a product, service, or process, and tangible includes things the customer can see, touch, feel, or experience.
content: Because every project creates some form of output, every project can and should use a WBS. The specific nature of the output may vary: a physical product, a service experience, or a repeatable process. Regardless of type, the WBS documents the components of that outcome. This makes the WBS the common scope-defining thread across product, service, and process creation projects.
relationships: WBS is the common scope artifact across project types
search_queries: Is WBS used for all projects?
Do service projects need a WBS?
Can a process project have a WBS?
common_questions: Why is the WBS relevant to every project?
Does a non-physical outcome still need a WBS?
Can services and processes be represented in a WBS?
can_answer: Whether every project should use a WBS
Why WBS applies beyond product projects
How deliverables exist in different project types
examples: A bicycle project, a wedding planning service project, and an office relocation process project can all be represented with WBS deliverables.
best_practices: Tailor the deliverable hierarchy to the type of outcome being produced.
Remember that experiential service components can still be represented as deliverables.
warnings: Assuming only physical products need a WBS can leave service or process projects underdefined.
limitations: Different project types may require different decomposition styles even though the WBS concept remains applicable.
troubleshooting: If stakeholders believe their project has no deliverables, ask what outcome the customer will receive or experience.
decision_rules: If a project produces any customer outcome, represent that outcome through deliverables in a WBS.
